moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with the queen's pawn, staking a claim in the center and launching the London System.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single central pawn on d4. White aims to build a solid d4-e3 pawn duo rather than an aggressive d4.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop to f4 before playing e3
Build a solid pawn triangle with d4-e3-c3
alternatives: Nf3 — a flexible move that can also lead to the London after Bf4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors our central claim, establishing a symmetrical pawn tension in the center. This is the most classical response, leading to a solid Queen's Pawn structure.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5 central pawns. Neither side has committed to a pawn exchange yet, maintaining.
plans: Maintain the d4-d5 tension rather than exchanging
Develop the bishop to f4 before locking it in with e3

moveAnnotations.annotation: The signature move of the London System! We develop the dark-squared bishop BEFORE playing e3, because after e3 this bishop would be locked behind its own pawns.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Still d4 vs d5. The key principle: always develop Bf4 before playing e3, or the bishop gets buried.
plans: Support e5 control and prevent Black from freeing their position with ...e5
After e3-Bd3, aim the bishop battery at Black's kingside
alternatives: Nf3 — also good, but risks Black playing ...Bf5 to contest the diagonal

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the king's knight to its most natural square, attacking the e4 square and preparing kingside castling. This is a standard developing move that keeps all of Black's options open.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. The d4-d5 tension remains.
plans: Continue development with ...e6 and ...Be7 or ...Bd6
Potentially break with ...c5 to challenge White's center

moveAnnotations.annotation: We solidify our pawn center by creating the famous London pawn triangle. The pawn on e3 supports d4, secures the Bf4's retreat path, and opens the f1-a6 diagonal for our light-squared bishop.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d4-e3 pawn duo forms a solid triangle with f2.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop to d3, aiming at Black's kingside
Play Nf3, Nbd2, c3 to complete development

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black solidifies the d5 pawn and opens the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. This creates a solid Stonewall-like pawn chain on d5-e6 that controls the center well.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has d5-e6, a solid but slightly passive structure.
plans: Try to activate the light-squared bishop via ...b6 and ...Bb7 or ...Ba6
Challenge White's center with ...c5

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the king's knight to its ideal square, reinforcing control of e5 and d4 while preparing to castle.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. White maintains the d4-e3 structure with pieces supporting it.
plans: Castle kingside next
Develop Bd3 and Nbd2 to complete the London setup
alternatives: Nd2 — less common but avoids blocking the f-pawn; however, Nf3 is more natural

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at our center with the most principled break. By attacking d4, Black aims to dissolve our central pawn chain and open lines for their pieces, especially the dark-squared bishop.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black challenges the d4-e3 chain with ...c5.
plans: Strike back with ...cxd4 to open the c-file
Develop pieces actively and target White's center

moveAnnotations.annotation: We reinforce d4 with the c-pawn, creating an ironclad pawn center that is very hard to break down. The c3 pawn completes our pawn triangle (c3-d4-e3) and takes all pressure off our center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The c3-d4-e3 pawn triangle is the hallmark London structure.
plans: Complete development with Bd3, Nbd2, and O-O
After full development, prepare e3-e4 to seize more space
alternatives: dxc5 — captures but gives up the center; not in the London spirit

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queen's knight to its natural square, adding another attacker to the d4 pawn and preparing to increase central pressure.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. Black has two pieces and the c5 pawn targeting d4, but White's pawn triangle holds.
plans: Increase pressure on d4 with ...Qb6 or ...Rc8
Prepare ...e5 to strike in the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the queen's knight to d2 rather than c3, which is a key London System idea.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. White's solid c3-d4-e3 triangle remains intact.
plans: Develop bishop to d3 next, completing the London setup
Reroute Nd2 to e5 if the square is available
alternatives: Bd3 — also excellent; developing order between Nbd2 and Bd3 is flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the dark-squared bishop to d6, directly challenging our London bishop on f4. This is one of the most critical moments in the London System: Black offers a trade of dark-squared bishops.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change yet, but if Bxd6 Qxd6 occurs, Black keeps a flexible structure.
plans: Exchange on f4 to try to play ...e5 with tempo
Use Bd6 to support central expansion with ...e5

moveAnnotations.annotation: We retreat the bishop to g3 rather than allowing the exchange on d6. This is the modern main line approach in the London.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. White's structure remains solid.
plans: Complete development with Bd3 and O-O
The Bg3 supports e5 control and a potential h4-h5 kingside pawn storm
alternatives: Bxd6 — trading is simpler but gives Black easy development; Bg3 keeps more te...

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, tucking the king into safety and connecting the rooks. This is a natural and necessary move.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. Black's king is now on g8 behind the f7-g7-h7 pawns.
plans: Use the rooks on the central files
Play ...e5 to challenge the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: We place our light-squared bishop on its ideal diagonal, aiming straight at Black's kingside and the critical h7 pawn.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's c3-d4-e3 triangle is complete with all pieces developed.
plans: Castle kingside on the next move
After castling, prepare e3-e4 to open the center with a space advantage

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop to b7, solving the problem of the hemmed-in c8 bishop that was locked behind the e6 pawn.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black adds b6, preparing a queenside fianchetto.
plans: Develop the bishop to b7 for central pressure
Play ...Bb7 followed by ...Qe7 and ...Rc8 or ...e5
alternatives: ...Qe7 — preparing ...e5 directly without spending time on the fianchetto

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the London System, the King's Indian Counterattack is a dangerous line you must be aware of.
Black plays Nf6. This is the natural continuation that leads into the warning line.
White plays Bf4. This is the natural continuation that leads into the warning line.
g6. The position is sharp and requires precise play from this point forward.
White plays Nf3. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
Bg7 by Black. The position is becoming uncomfortable — careful defense is needed.
White continues with e3. Watch out — a mistake here would be very costly.
d6. The position is sharp and requires precise play from this point forward.
White plays Be2. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
Black castles, but the position requires careful play.
White castles, but the position requires careful play.
Nbd7. The position is sharp and requires precise play from this point forward.
White plays h3. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
Qe8 by Black. The position is becoming uncomfortable — careful defense is needed.
White continues with c3. Watch out — a mistake here would be very costly.
e5. The position is sharp and requires precise play from this point forward.
White plays Bh2. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
Nh5. We're approaching the critical position. Pay close attention to the next moves — this is where the danger lies.
dxe5! This is the move that causes all the trouble.
dxe5. The damage is done — this is the result you want to prevent. Remember where the critical decision point was earlier in the line.
Opening with 1.d4 as always in the London. But in this move order — popularized by GothamChess and Hikaru — we delay Bf4 and play Nf3 first.
Black takes the classical approach with 1...d5, establishing central control. This is the most common reply and leads to the standard London structure.
Here's the key difference from the main line: 2.Nf3 before Bf4. This prevents Black from playing an early ...Bf5 or ...c5 with tempo against our bishop.
Black develops the kingside knight, controlling e4 and preparing to castle. A natural and solid response.
Now we play Bf4, the London signature move. By waiting until move 3, we've ensured Black has already committed their knight to f6, making it harder for them to harass our bishop with ...Qb6 or grab.
Black strikes at our d4 pawn with ...c5, the main way to challenge the London center. This is the most principled response, creating tension immediately.
We solidify the center with e3, forming the classic London pawn triangle. The d4 pawn is now well-supported, and we've opened the diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
Black develops the queenside knight to its most natural square, adding pressure to d4 and preparing to complete development.
We develop the knight to d2 rather than c3. This is a typical London choice — the knight on d2 supports e4 pushes and keeps the c-pawn free to play c3 to reinforce d4.
Black plays a solid ...e6, bolstering d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. The position is taking on a classical Queen's Gambit flavor.
Completing the London pawn triangle: d4-e3-c3. Our center is rock solid. This is the ideal structure — hard for Black to break down and easy for us to play behind.
Black develops the bishop to d6, directly challenging our Bf4. This is the critical moment — we need to decide whether to exchange bishops or retreat to g3.
We retreat to g3 rather than trading. The bishop on g3 is still active on the h2-b8 diagonal and prevents Black from getting the bishop pair.
Black castles, getting the king to safety. The position is balanced but White has a very clear plan: Bd3, O-O, and then look for e4 or a kingside buildup.
Developing the bishop to its ideal square. From d3 it aims at h7, creating a potential battery with the queen. This is the classic London attacking setup against a castled king.
Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop to b7, where it will pressure e4 and the long diagonal. A solid queenside development plan.

We castle and our setup is complete. The position is comfortable for White: solid center, well-placed pieces, and clear plans. We can aim for e4, Qe2 + e4, or build up on the kingside with Ne5 and f4.
Black completes the fianchetto. The bishop on b7 is well-placed on the long diagonal. This is the typical middlegame structure — both sides are fully developed and the strategic battle begins.
Starting with d4 as usual. This line covers what to do when Black challenges us with an early ...Qb6, targeting b2. Hikaru's specific recommendation for handling this annoying line.
Black plays the standard 1...d5. So far nothing unusual — the fireworks come later.
We use the modern Nf3-first move order again. This line works whether you play Nf3 or Bf4 first, but the Nf3 order is slightly more accurate.
Black develops the knight naturally. The ...Qb6 idea is coming soon.
We play our London bishop move. Black will now try to exploit the fact that this bishop has left b2 undefended.
Black strikes at the center with ...c5 first, preparing to bring the queen out with tempo.
We calmly reinforce d4. The b2 pawn looks weak, but we have a plan for that.
Black develops the knight, adding pressure to d4 and preparing the queen sortie.
We play c3 to shore up d4. This invites Black to play ...Qb6, which looks scary but we're ready for it.
Here it comes! Black attacks b2 with the queen. Many London players panic here, but we have a clean solution. Don't try to defend b2 passively — we counter-attack.

The key move! We meet the queen with our own queen, offering a trade. If Black takes on b3, we recapture with axb3 and our rook opens on the a-file — great for us. Most players will decline the trade.
Black pushes ...c4, chasing our queen away and grabbing queenside space. This looks aggressive but it actually helps us — Black's queen is now misplaced on b6 and the c4 pawn can become a target lat.
We reroute the queen to c2, a perfect square. From here the queen eyes h7 (once Bd3 comes) and stays connected to the queenside. The queen is much better here than it was on d1.
Black develops the bishop actively to f5, trying to challenge our queen's diagonal. This is Black's most natural development.
We drop the queen back to c1. It might look odd, but from c1 the queen connects to the Bf4, supports a future Nh4 hitting the Bf5, and keeps flexible options.
Black solidifies with ...e6, supporting d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. The position is balanced but White has a clear plan.
Developing the knight to d2. From here it can go to e4 or help with the e4 pawn break. Notice how despite the early queen adventures, our development is excellent.
Black develops the bishop modestly to e7. The queen on b6 is now looking a bit misplaced — it's not doing much and will need to find a better home soon.
We castle and complete development. White stands well: solid center, harmonious pieces, and clear plans. Look for Nh4 to trade off Black's Bf5, then aim for e4.
Black castles too. The opening phase is over. White's position is comfortable — the queen reroute to c1 was the key idea. From here we aim for Nh4xf5, then e4 to open the center with an advantage.
This is the typical resulting position from the Anti-Qb6 line. Black has rerouted the knight, but White's setup is harmonious. The plan is Nh4 to trade the bishops on f5, then push e4.
The Classical London setup — straightforward and easy to remember. This is Anna Cramling's recommended line: just nine half-moves that give you a complete, solid position.
Black plays the symmetrical 1...d5. This Classical line works best against this response, giving White a natural development scheme.
In the Classical version, we play Bf4 on move 2 — the traditional London move order. Get the bishop out before it gets locked behind the e3 pawn.
Black develops the knight to f6, the most natural square. It controls e4 and prepares kingside castling. Nothing surprising here.
We lock in the pawn structure with e3. The bishop is already on f4, so we're not blocking anything.
Black mirrors with ...e6, creating a solid pawn duo on d5 and e6. This is the most classical setup — both sides have secure, symmetrical centers.
We bring out the knight to f3, controlling e5 and d4. The knight and bishop work together to dominate the dark squares in the center. Three pieces developed and we're ready to castle.
Black develops the bishop to e7, a modest but solid square. It prepares castling and doesn't commit to any aggressive plan yet.
The final piece of the Classical setup! The bishop on d3 aims at h7, creating attacking potential against a castled king.
Opening with 1.d4. In this Carlsen-inspired move order, Black responds with 1...Nf6 instead of the usual 1...d5, but we proceed with our standard London setup regardless.
Black plays 1...Nf6, an Indian Defense move order. This delays ...d5 and keeps options open for a King's Indian or Nimzo-Indian setup. We stick to our London plan.
Developing the knight first, keeping the London flexible. This prevents Black from playing ...e5 with tempo and prepares Bf4 on the next move.
Black plays ...d5, transposing toward a standard London structure. Now the center is symmetrical and we continue with our setup.
The London bishop comes out to f4 before e3 locks it in. This is the defining move of the London System.
Black challenges the d4 pawn immediately with ...c5. This is the most principled response, creating central tension before White can lock things down.
Reinforcing d4 and completing the pawn chain. The bishop is already out on f4, so e3 doesn't trap it. We now have the classic London pawn triangle forming.
Black develops the knight to c6, adding pressure to d4. A natural and standard developing move in this structure.
Developing the knight to d2 rather than c3. This keeps the c-pawn free for c3, supporting d4, and the knight can reroute to e5 or support e4 later.
Black solidifies the d5 pawn with ...e6 and prepares to develop the dark-squared bishop. A solid, classical approach.
Completing the pawn triangle with d4-e3-c3. The center is now rock solid and very hard for Black to crack open. This is the ideal London structure.
Black develops the bishop to d6, directly challenging our Bf4. This forces us to make a decision about the dark-squared bishops.
Retreating to g3 rather than trading. The bishop stays active on the diagonal and if Black trades on g3, we recapture with hxg3, opening the h-file for attack.
Black castles kingside, getting the king to safety. Now we complete our development with Bd3 and O-O.
The bishop goes to its ideal square on d3, aiming at h7 and creating potential for a kingside battery with the queen. Classic London attacking setup.
Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop to b7, pressuring the long diagonal. A natural plan in this structure.
We castle and the setup is complete. All pieces are on ideal squares and we have a clear plan heading into the middlegame.
Black completes the fianchetto. The bishop on b7 pressures e4 and the long diagonal. Both sides are fully developed.
The key outpost move! Carlsen's favorite in this structure. The knight on e5 is a monster — it attacks c6, d7, f7, and supports a kingside attack.
Starting with 1.d4 as always. This line covers Hikaru's specific handling of Black's aggressive ...Qb6 response, which targets the b2 pawn after Bf4.
Black plays the standard 1...d5, establishing central presence. The ...Qb6 idea is coming soon.
Developing the knight first with the modern move order. This keeps things flexible and prepares Bf4 on the next move.
Black develops the kingside knight naturally, controlling e4.
The London bishop comes out to f4. Black will now look to exploit the fact that b2 is undefended with an early ...Qb6.
Black strikes at d4 with ...c5, preparing to follow up with ...Qb6 to attack b2 with tempo.
Reinforcing d4 with e3. The b2 pawn looks vulnerable but we have a concrete plan to deal with ...Qb6.
Black develops the knight to c6, adding pressure to d4 and preparing the queen sortie.
We play c3, completing the pawn triangle and inviting Black to try ...Qb6. We're fully prepared for it.
Black plays the aggressive ...Qb6, targeting b2. This looks threatening but Hikaru has a clean antidote.
The key response! We counter with Qb3, offering a queen trade. If Black takes on b3, axb3 opens the a-file for our rook. Most players decline.
Black pushes ...c4, chasing the queen and grabbing queenside space. This looks aggressive but it actually helps White — the c4 pawn overextends and the queen on b6 becomes misplaced.
We reroute to c2, an excellent square. The queen eyes h7 for future attacks and stays flexible. This is the first step of Hikaru's Qb3-Qc2-Qc1 maneuver.
Black develops the bishop actively to f5, challenging our queen's diagonal and trying to seize the initiative.
The queen drops back to c1, completing the maneuver. From c1 it connects to the Bf4, supports a future Nh4 hitting Bf5, and keeps all options open. Black's queenside expansion has been neutralized.
Black solidifies with ...e6, supporting d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. The position is stabilizing.
Developing the knight to d2. It can head to e4 or support a future e4 push. Despite the early queen adventures, White's development is harmonious.
Black develops the bishop modestly to e7, preparing to castle. The queen on b6 is looking increasingly misplaced.
We develop the bishop to e2 rather than d3, since the Bf5 controls the d3 square. The bishop on e2 is solid and supports a future kingside expansion.
Black castles, getting the king to safety. The opening phase is nearly complete.
We castle as well. White's position is harmonious with clear plans: Nh4 to trade the Bf5, then push e4 to open the center.
Black reroutes the knight, likely heading for b6 or f8. This is the resulting position from Hikaru's Anti-Qb6 system.
